body.label: thm:divisor-topology-closure-singleton

hypothesis.properties: For each n in X, the set B_n is defined by B_n = { k \cdot n \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} }.
hypothesis.statement: We generate a topology on X using the basis \mathcal{B} = \{ B_n \mid n \in X \}, where B_n = \{ k \cdot n \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} \} is the set of all multiples of n. This topology is called the divisor topology.

body.claim: In the divisor topology on X, the closure of the singleton set \{m\}, denoted \overline{\{m\}}, is the set of all divisors of m; that is, \overline{\{m\}} = D(m) := \{ d \in X \mid d \text{ divides } m \}.

proof.statement: Define D(m) := \{ d \in X \mid d \text{ divides } m \}.

proof.text: We prove the equality of sets by showing the two inclusions \overline{\{m\}} \subseteq D(m) and D(m) \subseteq \overline{\{m\}}.

content.assumption: Let x \in X be arbitrary such that x \in \overline{\{m\}}.

content.claim: If x \in \overline{\{m\}}, then every open set U of X with x \in U satisfies U \cap \{m\} \neq \emptyset; equivalently, every open neighborhood U of x contains m.

Since the topology on X is generated by the basis \mathcal{B}, the family of basic open sets B_n with x \in B_n forms a neighborhood basis at x. Hence, for every n \in X with x \in B_n, we must have m \in B_n.
content.proof_method: basic properties of a topology generated by a basis

content.claim: For n = x, we have x \in B_x.
Because x \in B_x and every basic neighborhood B_n of x must contain m, it follows that m \in B_x.
content.proof_method: application of the neighborhood-basis characterization of closure

content.claim: From m \in B_x and the definition B_x = \{ k \cdot x \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} \}, there exists k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} such that m = k \cdot x.
The equality m = k \cdot x means precisely that x divides m, so x \in D(m).
Since x \in \overline{\{m\}} was arbitrary, we have shown that \overline{\{m\}} \subseteq D(m).

content.assumption: Let x \in X be arbitrary with the property that x divides m.

content.claim: There exists \ell \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} such that m = \ell \cdot x.
To show x \in \overline{\{m\}}, it suffices to prove that every open set U of X with x \in U satisfies U \cap \{m\} \neq \emptyset, i.e., m \in U.

content.assumption: Let U be an open subset of X such that x \in U.

content.claim: Since the topology is generated by the basis \mathcal{B}, the set U can be written as a union of basic open sets B_n, and the condition x \in U implies that there exists n \in X such that x \in B_n \subseteq U.
content.proof_method: characterization of open sets in a basis-generated topology

content.claim: From x \in B_n and the definition B_n = \{ k \cdot n \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} \}, there exists k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1} such that x = k \cdot n.
Combining m = \ell \cdot x with x = k \cdot n, we obtain m = \ell \cdot (k \cdot n) = (\ell k) \cdot n.
content.proof_method: substitution and associativity of integer multiplication

content.claim: Since \ell k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 1}, the equality m = (\ell k) \cdot n shows that m \in B_n.
Because B_n \subseteq U and m \in B_n, we have m \in U.
Every open set U containing x contains m, so x \in \overline{\{m\}}. Since x \in D(m) was arbitrary, this proves D(m) \subseteq \overline{\{m\}}.

proof.claim: Combining the two inclusions, we conclude that \overline{\{m\}} = D(m) = \{ d \in X \mid d \text{ divides } m \}.
